items.span_text:
Algorithm 1: Adam, our proposed algorithm for stochastic optimization. See section 2 for details,
and for a slightly more efficient (but less clear) order of computation. g _ { t } ^ { 2 } indicates the elementwise
square g _ { t } \odot g _ { t } . Good default settings for the tested machine learning problems are \alpha = 0 . 0 0 1
\bar { \beta _ { 1 } } = 0 \bar { . } 9 , \beta _ { 2 } \bar { = } 0 . 999 and \epsilon = 1 0 ^ { - 8 } . All operations on vectors are element-wise. With \beta _ { 1 } ^ { t } and \beta _ { 2 } ^ { t }
we denote \beta _ { 1 } and \beta _ { 2 } to the power t .
Require: \alpha \mathrm { : } Stepsize
Require: \beta _ { 1 } , \bar { \beta _ { 2 } } \overset { - } { \in } [ 0 , 1 ) : Exponential decay rates for the moment estimates
Require: f ( \theta ) { \mathrm { : } } Stochastic objective function with parameters θ
Require: \theta _ { 0 } \colon Initial parameter vector
m _ { 0 } \gets 0 (Initialize 1 ^ { \mathrm { s t } } moment vector)
v _ { 0 } 0 (Initialize 2 ^ { \mathrm { n d } } moment vector)
t \gets 0 (Initialize timestep)
while \theta _ { t } not converged do
t \gets t + 1
g _ { t } \gets \nabla _ { \theta } f _ { t } ( \theta _ { t - 1 } ) (Get gradients w.r.t. stochastic objective at timestep t)
m _ { t } \gets \beta _ { 1 } \cdot m _ { t - 1 } \dot { + } \left( 1 \bar { - } \beta _ { 1 } \right) \cdot g _ { t } (Update biased first moment estimate)
v _ { t } \beta _ { 2 } \cdot v _ { t - 1 } + ( 1 - \beta _ { 2 } ) \cdot g _ { t } ^ { 2 } (Update biased second raw moment estimate)
\widehat { m } _ { t } \gets m _ { t } / ( 1 - \beta _ { 1 } ^ { t } ) (Compute bias-corrected first moment estimate)
\widehat { v } _ { t } \gets v _ { t } / ( 1 - \beta _ { 2 } ^ { t } ) (Compute bias-corrected second raw moment estimate)
\theta _ { t } \theta _ { t - 1 } - \alpha \cdot \widehat { m } _ { t } / ( \sqrt { v _ { t } } + \epsilon ) (Update parameters)
end while
return { \underline { { \theta _ { t } } } } (Resulting parameters)
items.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

items.title: Attention Is All You Need

final_answer.markdown:
Both numbers are correct — they refer to different scopes, not a genuine contradiction.

## The two claims, side by side

**Adam's recommended default (β₂ = 0.999)** — Kingma & Ba's Algorithm 1 caption states:

> "Good default settings for the tested machine learning problems are α = 0.001, β₁ = 0.9, **β₂ = 0.999** and ε = 10⁻⁸." [1]

Note the qualifier: these are defaults "for the tested machine learning problems" (logistic regression, multilayer MLP on MNIST, and a convolutional network). The value is an empirically chosen default on those small-scale benchmarks, not a universal prescription.

**Transformer's chosen value (β₂ = 0.98)** — Vaswani et al. (§5.3 Optimizer) state:

> "We used the Adam optimizer [20] with β₁ = 0.9, **β₂ = 0.98** and ε = 10⁻⁹." [2]

This is the authors' configuration for training the Transformer base/big models on WMT 2014 machine translation.

## Resolution

There is no actual conflict — the scope differs:

| | β₂ | ε | Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adam paper | 0.999 | 10⁻⁸ | Recommendation for the small-scale problems tested in Adam |
| Transformer paper | 0.98 | 10⁻⁹ | Empirical choice for Transformer training |

The Transformer paper explicitly cites Adam [2], so its authors knowingly depart from Kingma & Ba's default. Smaller β₂ values shorten the effective window of the second-moment moving average, which can matter when gradients are sparse or noisy in ways the original Adam benchmarks did not exercise. (The Transformer passage does not itself justify the choice — only states it.)

**Bottom line:** 0.999 is the Adam default; 0.98 is a task-specific override used by the Transformer authors. Both are true within their respective scopes.

Sources
[1] Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization, unsectioned, p. 2
[2] Attention Is All You Need, 5.3 Optimizer, p. 7

result.answer_guidance: State both claims, the scope difference, and the resulting conclusion.
result.evidence_standard: Use exact passages returned by read_locations for paper-content claims. Treat candidate metadata and location previews as navigation only, and state evidence gaps plainly.
instructions: Preserve qualifiers such as default, measured, recommended, and conditional.
Resolve by scope only when the evidence supports it.
result.when_to_use: Compare competing source claims and adjudicate or preserve their conflict.

locations.preview: The algorithm updates exponential moving averages of the gradient $( m _ { t } )$ and the squared gradient $( v _ { t } )$ where the hyper-parameters $\beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } \in [ 0 , 1 )$ control the exponential decay rates of these moving averages. The moving averages themselves are estimates of the $1 ^ { \mathrm { s t } }$ moment (the mean) and the $2 ^ { \mathrm { n d } }$ raw moment (the uncentered variance) of the gradient. However, these moving averages are initialized as (vectors
locations.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

locations.preview: An important property of Adam’s update rule is its careful choice of stepsizes. Assuming $\epsilon = 0 .$ , the effective step taken in parameter space at timestep t is $\Delta _ { t } = \alpha \cdot \widehat { m } _ { t } / \sqrt { \widehat { v } _ { t } }$ . The effective stepsize has two upper bounds: $| \Delta _ { t } ^ { \bullet } | \le \alpha \cdot ( 1 \stackrel { \bullet } { - } \beta _ { 1 } ) / \sqrt { 1 - \beta _ { 2 } }$ in the case $( 1 - \beta _ { 1 } ) > \sqrt { 1 - \beta _ { 2 } }
locations.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

locations.preview: As explained in section 2, Adam utilizes initialization bias correction terms. We will here derive the term for the second moment estimate; the derivation for the first moment estimate is completely analogous. Let $g$ be the gradient of the stochastic objective $f ,$ and we wish to estimate its second raw moment (uncentered variance) using an exponential moving average of the squared gradient, with decay rate $\beta _ { 2 }$ . Let $g _ { 1 } , . . . , g _ { T }$ be the gradients at subsequent ti
locations.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

locations.preview: where $\zeta = 0$ if the true second moment $\mathbb { E } [ g _ { i } ^ { 2 } ]$ is stationary; otherwise $\zeta$ can be kept small since the exponential decay rate $\beta _ { 1 }$ can (and should) be chosen such that the exponential moving average assigns small weights to gradients too far in the past. What is left is the term $( 1 - \beta _ { 2 } ^ { t } )$ which is caused by initializing the running average with zeros. In algorithm 1 we therefore divide by this term to correct the initializa
locations.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

locations.preview: where $\begin{array} { r } { \theta ^ { * } = \arg \operatorname* { m i n } _ { \theta \in \mathcal { X } } \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { T } f _ { t } ( \theta ) } \end{array}$ . We show Adam has $O ( \sqrt { T } )$ regret bound and a proof is given in the appendix. Our result is comparable to the best known bound for this general convex online learning problem. locations.preview: We used the Adam optimizer [20] with $\beta _ { 1 } = 0 . 9 , \beta _ { 2 } = 0 . 9 8$ and $\epsilon = 1 0 ^ { - 9 }$ . We varied the learning rate over the course of training, according to the formula:
locations.title: Attention Is All You Need

locations.preview: This corresponds to increasing the learning rate linearly for the first warmup\_steps training steps, and decreasing it thereafter proportionally to the inverse square root of the step number. We used warmup $. s t e p s = 4 0 0 0$
locations.title: Attention Is All You Need

locations.preview: Label Smoothing During training, we employed label smoothing of value $\epsilon _ { l s } = 0 . 1 [ 3 6 ]$ . This hurts perplexity, as the model learns to be more unsure, but improves accuracy and BLEU score.
locations.title: Attention Is All You Need

locations.preview: On the WMT 2014 English-to-French translation task, our big model achieves a BLEU score of 41.0, outperforming all of the previously published single models, at less than 1/4 the training cost of the previous state-of-the-art model. The Transformer (big) model trained for English-to-French used dropout rate $P _ { d r o p } = 0 . 1$ , instead of 0.3.
locations.title: Attention Is All You Need

locations.preview: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. The best performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being mor
locations.title: Attention Is All You Need

locations.preview: Recurrent models typically factor computation along the symbol positions of the input and output sequences. Aligning the positions to steps in computation time, they generate a sequence of hidden states $h _ { t } ,$ as a function of the previous hidden state $h _ { t - 1 }$ and the input for position t. This inherently sequential nature precludes parallelization within training examples, which becomes critical at longer sequence lengths, as memory constraints limit batching across examples. Rec
locations.title: Attention Is All You Need

locations.preview: Self-attention, sometimes called intra-attention is an attention mechanism relating different positions of a single sequence in order to compute a representation of the sequence. Self-attention has been used successfully in a variety of tasks including reading comprehension, abstractive summarization, textual entailment and learning task-independent sentence representations [4, 27, 28, 22].
locations.title: Attention Is All You Need

locations.preview: We also experimented with using learned positional embeddings [9] instead, and found that the two versions produced nearly identical results (see Table 3 row (E)). We chose the sinusoidal version because it may allow the model to extrapolate to sequence lengths longer than the ones encountered during training.
locations.title: Attention Is All You Need
